title: Still on Track for a Good Year | Dimensional
gist: Despite a sharp single-day S&P 500 drop and an ongoing war backdrop, the index is up 8.4% cumulatively in early 2026, ahead of pace for a strong year; the takeaway is that tolerating volatility is the price investors pay to earn the equity premium.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 Index dropped 2.63% on June 5, 2026, placing it among the worst daily returns for the index over the past 30 years.
insights.speaker: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: A historically rare drawdown day did not derail an otherwise strong year, a reminder not to overweight single-day moves.
insights.quote: The S&P 500 Index dropped 2.63% on June 5, placing it among the worst of the index’s daily returns over the past 30 years.
insights.locator: I’ve said many times in this space

insights.claim: The S&P 500 Index posted a cumulative return of 8.4% over the first 112 trading days of 2026, ahead of the longer-term annualized return of 10.6%.
insights.speaker: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Year-to-date equity returns are tracking above the long-run average, supporting a stay-invested posture.
insights.quote: A cumulative return of 8.4% is a good start to the year considering the longer-term annualized return has been 10.6%.
insights.locator: But what doesn’t stink is zooming out

insights.claim: Strong 2026 equity returns came against the backdrop of an ongoing war.
insights.speaker: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Markets can advance despite geopolitical conflict, cautioning against selling on war headlines alone.
insights.quote: especially if accompanied by the knowledge that we’d have an ongoing war as a backdrop
insights.locator: Most of us would have gladly signed up

insights.claim: Checking account statements less frequently helps investors weather market volatility without panic-driven decisions.
insights.speaker: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Reducing the frequency of portfolio monitoring is a low-cost behavioral lever to avoid selling into drawdowns.
insights.quote: the secret to weathering market volatility is leaving your account statements unopened more often
insights.locator: I’ve said many times in this space

insights.claim: The smoothness of return delivery, not the magnitude, is what distracts investors from the long-term view; the same cumulative return delivered steadily would feel far more reassuring.
insights.speaker: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Framing volatility as path-dependent noise can help investors stay the course through bumpy returns.
insights.quote: The ride has been bumpy, but that’s what distracts from the long-term view.

insights.claim: Tolerating short-term volatility is part of what earns investors the equity premium, the return difference between stocks and a risk-free asset such as short-term Treasury bills.
insights.speaker: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Volatility is the compensated risk behind equity returns, so enduring it is a feature, not a bug, of the strategy.
insights.quote: tolerating the bumps is part of what earns you the equity premium
insights.locator: If we had received the same cumulative return

source_meta.author: Wes Crill, PhD, Senior Client Solutions Director and Vice President
